(関東地方, *Kantō Chihō*; IPA: [[kaꜜn.toː, kan.toː tɕiꜜ.hoː, kan.toː tɕi̥.hoꜜː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")) is a [geographical](/wiki/Geography "Geography") [region](/wiki/Region "Region") of [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), the largest island of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In a common definition, the region includes the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") and encompasses seven [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan"): [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture"), [Gunma](/wiki/Gunma_Prefecture "Gunma Prefecture"), [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture"), [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa_Prefecture "Kanagawa Prefecture"), [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture"), [Tochigi](/wiki/Tochigi_Prefecture "Tochigi Prefecture"), and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). Slightly more than 45 percent of the land area within its boundaries is the [Kantō Plain](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_Plain "Kantō Plain"). The rest consists of the hills and mountains that form land borders with other [regions of Japan](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Japan "List of regions of Japan").

The **Kantō region** (関東地方, *Kantō Chihō*; IPA: [[kaꜜn.toː, kan.toː tɕiꜜ.hoː, kan.toː tɕi̥.hoꜜː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")) is a [geographical](/wiki/Geography "Geography") [region](/wiki/Region "Region") of [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), the largest island of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In a common definition, the region includes the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") and encompasses seven [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan"): [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture"), [Gunma](/wiki/Gunma_Prefecture "Gunma Prefecture"), [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture"), [Kanagawa](/wiki/Kanagawa_Prefecture "Kanagawa Prefecture"), [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_Prefecture "Saitama Prefecture"), [Tochigi](/wiki/Tochigi_Prefecture "Tochigi Prefecture"), and [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). Slightly more than 45 percent of the land area within its boundaries is the [Kantō Plain](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_Plain "Kantō Plain"). The rest consists of the hills and mountains that form land borders with other [regions of Japan](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Japan "List of regions of Japan").

As the Kantō region contains Tokyo, the capital and largest city of Japan, the region is considered the center of Japan's politics and economy. According to the official census on October 1, 2010, by the [Statistics Bureau of Japan](/wiki/Statistics_Bureau_(Japan) "Statistics Bureau (Japan)"), the population was 42,607,376,[[3]](#cite_note-statb1-3) amounting to approximately one third of the total population of Japan.

## Other definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Other definitions")]

The Kantō regional governors' association (関東地方知事会, *Kantō chihō chijikai*) assembles the prefectural governors of Ibaraki, Tochigi, Gunma, Saitama, Chiba, Tokyo, Kanagawa, Yamanashi, Nagano, and Shizuoka.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4)[[5]](#cite_note-auto3-5)

The Kantō Regional Development Bureau (関東地方整備局, *Kantō chihō seibi-kyoku*) of the [Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Land,_Infrastructure,_Transport_and_Tourism "Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism") in the national government is responsible for eight prefectures generally (Ibaraki, Tochigi, Gunma, Saitama, Chiba, Tokyo, Kanagawa, Yamanashi) and parts of the waterways in two others (Nagano and Shizuoka).[[6]](#cite_note-auto2-6)

The Kantō [Bureau of Economy, Trade and Industry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economy,_Trade_and_Industry "Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry") (関東経済産業局, *Kantō keizai-sangyō-kyoku*) is responsible for eleven prefectures: Ibaraki, Tochigi, Gunma, Saitama, Chiba, Tokyo, Kanagawa, Niigata, Yamanashi, Nagano and Shizuoka.[[7]](#cite_note-auto1-7)

In the police organization of Japan, the [National Police Agency](/wiki/National_Police_Agency_(Japan) "National Police Agency (Japan)")'s supervisory office for Kantō (関東管区警察局, *Kantō kanku keisatsu-kyoku*) is responsible for the [Prefectural police departments](/wiki/Prefectural_police_department "Prefectural police department") of Ibaraki, Tochigi, Gunma, Saitama, Chiba, Kanagawa, Niigata, Yamanashi, Nagano and Shizuoka.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Tokyo is not part of Kantō or any NPA region, its police has a dedicated liaison office with the national agency of its own.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Kanto_Region_Japan_2003.png/250px-Kanto_Region_Japan_2003.png)](/wiki/File:Kanto_Region_Japan_2003.png)

Kantō region satellite photo

The surface geology of the Kantō Plain is the Quaternary alluvium and diluvium. The low mountain vegetation at an altitude of about 500 to 900 m in and around the plain is an evergreen broad-leaved forest zone. The distribution height range of [laurel forests](/wiki/Laurel_forest "Laurel forest") is 900 m in Hakone, about 800 m in Tanzawa and Takao, about 700 m in Okutama, Oku Musashi and Oku Chichibu, about 600 m in Nishijoshu, Akagiyama, Ashio Mountains and Tsukuba Mountains and about 500 m in Kitage and [Nasu Mountains](/wiki/Nasu_Mountains "Nasu Mountains").

Over the evergreen broad-leaved forest are deciduous broad-leaved forests such as beech, birch, and *[Quercus crispula](/wiki/Quercus_crispula "Quercus crispula")*. In addition, coniferous forests such as *[Abies veitchii](/wiki/Abies_veitchii "Abies veitchii")* and *[Betula ermanii](/wiki/Betula_ermanii "Betula ermanii")* spread above the deciduous broad-leaved forest from an altitude of about 1100 m higher than the lower limit of the deciduous broad-leaved forest.

Mountains are spread out such as the Taishaku Mountains, [Mt. Takahara](/wiki/Mt._Takahara "Mt. Takahara"), Mt. Nasu, and Mt. Yamizo. The Kantō Plain, which is the largest plain in Japan. Just north of the Enna Hills is Japan's largest alluvial fan Nasuno at the foot of Mt. The Kujukuri Plain. The southern part of Chiba Prefecture is the Boso hills. The area around Kasumigaura in Ibaraki Prefecture is the Joso plateau and Hitachi plateau. Gunma Prefecture and the Chichibu region of Saitama Prefecture are basins. Rivers such as the Arakawa and Edo rivers pour into Tokyo Bay, and the Kinugawa and Tone rivers flow into the Pacific Ocean in Inubōsaki.

Tokyo Bay is surrounded by the Boso Peninsula and the Miura Peninsula, facing the west side of Chiba Prefecture, a part of Tokyo and the east side of Kanagawa Prefecture, and borders the Pacific Ocean from Uraga Suido. The coastal area is an industrial area. The south side of Kanagawa Prefecture faces Sagami Bay and Sagami Nada. The southern coast of Ibaraki Prefecture faces Kashima Nada. The Sagami Trough, which was the epicenter of the two Kantō earthquakes, passes through Sagami Bay. Efforts are being made to take safety measures against earthquakes in various places.

The highest point is the summit of Mt. Nikko-Shirane (Mt. Oku-Shirane) on the border between Nikko City, Tochigi Prefecture and Katashina Village, Gunma Prefecture. It is the eighth highest point in Japan's prefectures. It is also the highest point north of Kantō (Kantō, Tōhoku, Hokkaido). The highest points of the prefectures are Mt. Sanpo (2,483 m) in Saitama, Mt. Kumotori (2,017 m) in Tokyo, Mt. Hiru (1,673 m) in Kanagawa, Mt. Yamizo (1,022 m) in Ibaraki, and Mt. Atago (408 m) in Chiba. Atagoyama in Chiba Prefecture is the lowest among the highest peaks in each prefecture.

The region experiences a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") with a summer to fall precipitation maximum (*Cfa/Cwa*).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The name Kantō literally means "East of the Barrier". The name *Kantō* is nowadays generally considered to mean the region east (東) of the [Hakone Barrier](/wiki/Hakone_Barrier "Hakone Barrier") (箱根関). The antonym of Kantō is the [Kansai region](/wiki/Kansai_region "Kansai region") meaning "West of the Barrier", which lies in western Honshu and was the center of feudal Japan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Mt_Nikko.jpeg/250px-Mt_Nikko.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Mt_Nikko.jpeg)

[Mount Nikkō-Shirane](/wiki/Mount_Nikk%C5%8D-Shirane "Mount Nikkō-Shirane") in the Kantō region

[Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura "Kamakura") was the political capital during the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") and it served as the seat of the [Kamakura shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate") from 1185 to 1333, established by Minamoto no Yoritomo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/TsurugaokaHachiman-M8867.jpg/250px-TsurugaokaHachiman-M8867.jpg)](/wiki/File:TsurugaokaHachiman-M8867.jpg)

[Tsurugaoka Hachiman](/wiki/Tsurugaoka_Hachimang%C5%AB "Tsurugaoka Hachimangū") in Kamakura

This was the first military government in Japan's history. Kamakura flourished until the fall of the [Kamakura Shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_Shogunate "Kamakura Shogunate"), and its political functions returned to [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") in 1392.

In 1591, [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") gave up control of his five provinces ([Mikawa](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province"), [Tōtōmi](/wiki/T%C5%8Dt%C5%8Dmi_Province "Tōtōmi Province"), [Suruga](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province"), [Shinano](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province"), and [Kai](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province")) and moved all his soldiers and vassals to his new eight provinces in the Kantō region. The proclamation of this decision happened on the same day Toyotomi Hideyoshi, the de facto ruler of Japan at that time, entered Odawara castle following the surrender of the [Hōjō clan](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Hōjō clan") after the [Siege of Odawara (1590)](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)").[[9]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-9) The moment Ieyasu was appointed to rule Kantō, he immediately assign his premier vassals such as [Ii Naomasa](/wiki/Ii_Naomasa "Ii Naomasa"), [Honda Tadakatsu](/wiki/Honda_Tadakatsu "Honda Tadakatsu"), [Sakakibara Yasumasa](/wiki/Sakakibara_Yasumasa "Sakakibara Yasumasa"), and Sakai Ietsugu, son of Sakai Tadatsugu, each to control large area of the former [Hōjō clan](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Hōjō clan") territories in Kantō. Historian such as Kawamura saw this step was meant to bring order the newly subdued population of the area, while also guarding the eastern domains from the influence or threat from the [Satomi clan](/wiki/Satomi_clan "Satomi clan") which had not yet submit to the rule of Toyotomi at that time.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The governors of Kantō region under Ieyasu rule:

Ieyasu established his personal new seat of power on [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo") town, which at that time was an underdeveloped town in Kantō.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENakamura2010210-43)[[a]](#cite_note-46)

In the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), Kantō became the center of modern development. Within the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") and especially the Tokyo-[Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama") metropolitan area, the Kantō plain houses not only Japan's seat of government but also the nation's largest group of universities and cultural institutions, the greatest population and a large industrial zone. Although most of the Kantō plain is used for residential, commercial or industrial construction, some land is still farmed. Rice is the principal crop, although the zone around Tokyo and Yokohama has been landscaped to grow garden produce for the metropolitan market.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Operation Coronet](/wiki/Operation_Downfall#Coronet "Operation Downfall"), part of [Operation Downfall](/wiki/Operation_Downfall "Operation Downfall"), the proposed Allied invasion of Japan during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), was scheduled to land on the [Kantō Plain](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_Plain "Kantō Plain").

### Great earthquake of 1923

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great earthquake of 1923")]

A watershed moment of Japan's modern history took place in the late Taishō period: the [Great Kantō earthquake of 1923](/wiki/1923_Great_Kant%C5%8D_earthquake "1923 Great Kantō earthquake"). The quake, which claimed more than 100,000 lives and ravaged Greater Tokyo area, occurred at a time when Japan was still reeling from the economic recession in reaction to the high-flying years during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the earthquake, many people in Kantō started creating art with different varieties of colors. They made art of earthquake and small towns to symbolize the small towns destroyed in the quake.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

### North and south

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: North and south")]

The most often used subdivision of the region is dividing it to "North Kantō" (北関東, *Kita-Kantō*), consisting of Ibaraki, Tochigi, and Gunma prefectures, and "South Kantō" (南関東, *Minami-Kantō*), consisting of Saitama (sometimes classified North),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] Chiba, [Tokyo Metropolis](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") (sometimes singulated),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and Kanagawa prefectures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] South Kantō is often regarded as [synonymous](/wiki/Synonym "Synonym") with the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area"). As part of Japan's attempts to [predict earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake_prediction "Earthquake prediction"), an area roughly corresponding to South Kantō has been designated an 'Area of Intensified Observation' by the [Coordinating Committee for Earthquake Prediction](/wiki/Coordinating_Committee_for_Earthquake_Prediction "Coordinating Committee for Earthquake Prediction").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

The Japanese [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_Japan "House of Representatives of Japan")' divides it into the North Kantō (北関東, *Kita-Kantō*) electorate which consists of Ibaraki, Tochigi, Gunma, and Saitama prefectures, Tokyo electorate, and the South Kantō (南関東, *Minami-Kantō*) electorate which consists of Chiba, Kanagawa, and [Yamanashi](/wiki/Yamanashi_Prefecture "Yamanashi Prefecture") prefectures (note that Yamanashi is out of the Kantō region in the orthodox definition).

[Keirin](/wiki/Keirin "Keirin")'s South Kantō (南関東, *Minami-Kantō*) consists of Chiba, Kanagawa, and [Shizuoka](/wiki/Shizuoka_Prefecture "Shizuoka Prefecture") prefectures.

### East and west

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: East and west")]

This division is not often but sometimes used.

* East Kantō (東関東, *Higashi-Kantō*): Ibaraki, Tochigi, and Chiba prefectures.
* West Kantō (西関東, *Nishi-Kantō*): Gunma, Saitama, Tokyo, Kanagawa (and sometimes Yamanashi) prefectures.

### Inland and coastal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Inland and coastal")]

This division is sometimes used in economics and geography. The border can be modified if the topography is taken for prefectural boundaries.

* Inland Kantō (関東内陸部, *Kantō nairiku-bu*): Tochigi, Gunma, Saitama (and sometimes Yamanashi) prefectures.
* Coastal Kantō (関東沿岸部, *Kantō engan-bu*): Ibaraki, Chiba, Tokyo, and Kanagawa prefectures.

### Greater Kantō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Greater Kantō")]

The Japanese national government defines the [National Capital Region](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area#National_Capital_Region "Greater Tokyo Area") (首都圏, *Shuto-ken*) as the Kantō region plus Yamanashi Prefecture. Japan's national public broadcaster [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") uses Kantō-kō-shin-etsu (関東甲信越) involving Yamanashi, Nagano, and Niigata prefectures for regional programming and administration.

The Kantō region is the most highly developed, urbanized, and industrialized part of Japan. [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama") form a single industrial complex with a concentration of light and heavy industry along [Tokyo Bay](/wiki/Tokyo_Bay "Tokyo Bay"). Other major cities in the area include [Kawasaki](/wiki/Kawasaki,_Kanagawa "Kawasaki, Kanagawa") (in Kanagawa Prefecture); [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama,_Saitama "Saitama, Saitama") (in Saitama Prefecture); and [Chiba](/wiki/Chiba,_Chiba "Chiba, Chiba") (in Chiba Prefecture). Smaller cities, farther away from the coast, house substantial light and automotive industries. The average population density reached 1,192 persons per square kilometer in 1991.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kant%C5%8D_region&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

The Kantō region largely corresponds to the Tokyo Metropolitan Area with the exception that it does not contain Yamanashi prefecture.

The Tokyo Metropolitan Area has the largest city economy in the world and is one of the major global center of trade and commerce along with New York City, Los Angeles, Shanghai, Paris, Seoul, and London.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** Historian Adam Sadler saw this step as the riskiest move Ieyasu ever made—to leave his home province and rely on the uncertain loyalty of the formerly Hōjō clan samurai in Kantō. In the end however, it worked out brilliantly for Ieyasu. He reformed the Kantō region, controlled and pacified the Hōjō samurai and improved the underlying economic infrastructure of the lands. Also, because Kantō was somewhat isolated from the rest of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), Ieyasu was able to ally with daimyos of north-east Japan such as [Date Masamune](/wiki/Date_Masamune "Date Masamune"), [Mogami Yoshiaki](/wiki/Mogami_Yoshiaki "Mogami Yoshiaki"), [Satake Yoshishige](/wiki/Satake_Yoshishige "Satake Yoshishige") and [Nanbu Nobunao](/wiki/Nanbu_Nobunao "Nanbu Nobunao"); he was also able to maintain a unique level of autonomy from [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi")'s rule. Within a few years, Ieyasu had become the second most powerful [daimyo](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo") in Japan. It was said by anecdotal proverb that: "Ieyasu won the Empire by retreating."[[44]](#cite_note-44) Historian Watanabe Daimon stated that the general opinion was that Ieyasu was reluctant about his transfer to Kantō. However, Daimon stated that thinking Ieyasu was reluctant was an opinion of a later era. Daimon suspected that Ieyasu actually saw this transfer positively as he realised a huge undeveloped potential by making Edo as his seat of power.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Historian [Andō yūichirō](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%AE%89%E8%97%A4%E5%84%AA%E4%B8%80%E9%83%8E "jp:安藤優一郎") further added, the true intention of Hideyoshi transferring Ieyasu to Kantō was to weaken the power of the Tokugawa clan by moving them from their ancestral land in Mikawa, as he expected the former Hōjō vassals in Kantō would rebel against Ieyasu. However, this backfired as Ieyasu not only doubled the territories he control, but he also further added the bulk of new vassals in Kantō to the already impressive political and military power of Tokugawa regime as they already absorbed the army of Imagawa clan and Takeda clan before.[[9]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-9)
